◆ ADVANCED USER SKILL · /efficient-team · JULY 2026

Two agents, both in
efficient mode, one switch.

/efficient-team puts Claude Code and OpenAI Codex to work together — each keeping its most capable model on the judgment calls and pushing the token-heavy grunt work down to cheaper models. Same quality ceiling. On the work that gets routed, a fraction of the cost.

Codex numbers are parsed from the real rollout logs of a routed run. Claude numbers are the same mechanism, priced from verified rates — labeled projected, never invented.

See the cost math Does output suffer?

measured saving on the routed Codex work
2
agents, one command
5×
cheapest-tier price gap, each agent
55–60%
projected on a normal project
What "efficient team" means

Two disciplines,
armed by one command.

◆

Claude half — efficient-opus

Opus 4.8 stays the conductor: architecture, integration, final review. Search sweeps, file inventory, and bounded edits get delegated to Sonnet and Haiku subagents that cost a fraction as much.

◈

Codex half — efficient-codex

GPT-5.6 Sol stays on security, architecture, and adversarial review. Mechanical sweeps route to Luna (5× cheaper), everyday engineering to Terra (2× cheaper) — chosen per call.

One switch

/efficient-team loads the Claude discipline and arms Codex tier-routing for every call. efficient team off stands it back down. You pick your own main model — a skill can't switch it for you.

The price gap that pays for it

Most tokens are input.
All of them bill at your top model's rate.

Both agents have the same shape

In agentic work the bulk of what you pay for is input — file reads, tool output, logs — billed at whatever model is running. Move that bulk to a cheaper tier and it costs 2× or 5× less, on either agent. The flagship only ever sees distilled evidence.

See the arithmetic →
# per million tokens, in / out CLAUDE CODEX Opus 4.8 $5 / $25 Sol $5 / $30 flagship Sonnet 5 $3 / $15 Terra $2.50 / $15 ~2x cheaper Haiku 4.5 $1 / $5 Luna $1 / $6 5x cheaper → conductor thinks · cheap tiers absorb the reading
Sol · flagship
$5 / $30
Terra · everyday
2× cheaper
Luna · mechanical
5× cheaper

Codex GPT-5.6 tiers. The Claude side has the identical shape — Opus over Sonnet over Haiku — so the same routing logic pays off on both agents.

The math — measured on Codex, projected on Claude

What routing actually
saves you.

Codex — measured from real rollout logs

Real Codex calls from a routed run's rollout logs. Sol kept the heavy reviews (correct — they needed Sol); the mechanical and everyday work routed down to Terra and Luna.

# parsed from ~/.codex/sessions rollout logs gpt-5.6-sol 32 calls 5.30M tok reviews, diagnosis gpt-5.6-terra 4 calls 75.8k tok build / verify gpt-5.6-luna 12 calls 258.5k tok sweeps, inventory → 334k tok routed off Sol · $3.76 → $1.01 · 73% saved
Measured saving on the routed Codex work

The 16 calls that routed to Terra + Luna moved 334k tokens off Sol. At Sol rates they'd have cost $3.76; routed, they cost $1.01 — 73% cheaper on that work, no quality lost, because none of it was judgment work.

Honest note — not a whole-project 73%

Sol did the bulk in this run because the work was review-heavy, and those reviews genuinely needed Sol. The 73% is the real saving on the portion that was safe to route. For a typical project with more mechanical work, the projection is below.

Claude — same mechanism, projected

Opus 4.8 conducts while bounded work is delegated to Sonnet subagents, exactly as the skill prescribes. There's no isolated Opus-only baseline to diff against, so we don't fake a measured Claude percentage — we price the same routing from the verified rates and label it projected.

# same routing, Claude rates, 1M mixed tokens All-Opus 1.0M @ $5-tier = $10.00 baseline Routed 60/25/15 Haiku+Sonnet+Opus = $4.20 projected · 58% off → conductor stays on Opus · bulk absorbed cheaper
Codex · All-Sol
$11.25
Codex · routed 60/25/15
$4.44
Codex · routed 50/30/20
$5.06
Claude · All-Opus
$10.00
Claude · routed 60/25/15
$4.20
flagship baseline Codex routed Claude routed

Mix = share of tokens going to the cheap / mid / flagship tiers. Codex rows priced per 1M tokens at Sol $5/$30 · Terra $2.50/$15 · Luna $1/$6; Claude rows at Opus $5/$25 · Sonnet $3/$15 · Haiku $1/$5 (verified July 2026). Codex 73% is measured; the projected rows are estimates from real prices, not guarantees — your workload decides your number. A tiny fix delegates nothing and saves nothing; the skill says skip it there.

The second saver

Context stays lean,
and the saving compounds.

Where the heavy reading happens matters

Cheaper models are only half of it. When a subagent reads a big file or a long log, that bulk lands in its throwaway context and never enters the main window. A bloated window isn't a one-time cost — it's re-processed on every single turn.

Without

Main model reads everything → the window fills with logs and file dumps → every later turn re-pays for all of it.

→
With subagents

Subagent ingests the bulk, returns a short answer → the main window stays small.

→
Payoff

A small, stable main context stays cached and avoids compaction — so the saving compounds every turn.

Why it compounds

Cheaper models cut the rate; lean context cuts the volume. Keeping the window small is the difference between paying for a giant context once versus dozens of times.

The honest part

Does the output
stay the same?

✓

Holds or improves

The most capable model stays on every judgment call. Cheap models only touch bounded, verifiable work, and the premium model checks their output before it's used.

◈

A real second opinion

Two independent agents beat one. Run one as builder and the other as an adversarial reviewer and it catches real bugs a single-agent path would ship — quality you don't get from one model alone.

⚠

The real risk

Route judgment work to a cheap tier and quality drops. That's the failure mode. So the routing discipline — cheap for mechanical, premium for judgment — is the quality control.

Bottom line

The savings come from moving mechanical work to cheaper models, never judgment. Respect that line and you don't trade quality away — you add an independent reviewer on top. Cross it and you lose both.

No trust required

Verify any session
yourself.

Paste this in the terminal — it reports each Codex call's tier and tokens from the rollout logs.

python3 - <<'PY'
import json,glob,os,collections
t=collections.defaultdict(lambda:[0,0])
for f in glob.glob(os.path.expanduser('~/.codex/sessions/*/*/*/rollout-*.jsonl')):
    model=toks=None
    for line in open(f):
        try:o=json.loads(line)
        except:continue
        p=o.get('payload',{})
        if o.get('type')=='turn_context' and p.get('model'): model=p['model']
        if p.get('type')=='token_count':
            ti=p.get('info',{}).get('total_token_usage',{}); toks=ti.get('total_tokens')
    if model and model.startswith('gpt-5.6'):
        t[model][0]+=1; t[model][1]+=toks or 0
for k,v in sorted(t.items()): print(f'{k}: {v[0]} calls, {v[1]:,} tokens')
PY
